content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you ___ (mix) red and blue paint, you get purple.
distractor_rationale: will in the if-clause is incorrect in zero conditional — this is a universal truth, not a future event
past simple cannot express a timeless scientific fact that is always true
present continuous cannot state a general rule that applies every time without exception

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Tom forgets his umbrella tomorrow, he ___ (get) wet on the way to work.
distractor_rationale: present simple in the result clause turns this into a zero conditional universal truth, but 'tomorrow' marks a specific future event requiring will
past simple cannot express a future result that depends on a condition happening tomorrow
present continuous cannot express a future result clause in a first conditional — will + base verb is required

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Plants always die if you ___ (not water) them regularly.
distractor_rationale: won't in the if-clause is a classic error — the if-clause of zero conditional requires present simple, not will
past simple cannot express a timeless general truth about what always happens to plants
present continuous cannot state a habitual condition that applies universally every time

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the weather is good this weekend, we ___ (have) a barbecue in the garden.
distractor_rationale: present simple in the result clause would make this a zero conditional universal truth, but the specific time marker 'this weekend' shows it is a realistic future plan requiring will
would have belongs to the second conditional for hypothetical or unlikely situations, not a realistic future possibility
present continuous alone cannot serve as the result clause of a first conditional — will + base verb is the required structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you ___ (leave) metal objects outside in the rain, they rust within a few days.
distractor_rationale: will in the if-clause is incorrect — this describes a universal physical process, so both clauses take present simple in the zero conditional
past simple cannot express a timeless general truth about what always happens to metal in rain
present perfect in the if-clause cannot state a repeated general condition that applies every time without exception

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Unless Sara calls me before noon, I ___ (assume) she is not coming to the meeting.
distractor_rationale: present simple in the result clause would make this a zero conditional fact, but 'before noon' marks a specific future situation requiring will
past simple cannot express a future result that depends on whether Sara calls or not
would assume belongs to the second conditional for hypothetical situations, but this is a realistic future possibility expressed with unless

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Every time the office printer runs out of paper, the display ___ (show) a red warning light.
distractor_rationale: will show is incorrect here because 'every time' introduces a zero conditional describing a consistent mechanical fact, not a specific future event
past simple cannot describe a repeated present truth that happens automatically each time the paper runs out
present continuous cannot state a universal mechanical rule — it would imply the light is showing right now, not always

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the manager ___ (approve) the budget proposal at Friday's meeting, the team will start the project next month.
distractor_rationale: will in the if-clause is the most common first conditional error — the if-clause requires present simple even though the event is in the future
past simple cannot express a future condition that will be decided at Friday's meeting
present perfect in the if-clause shifts meaning to a completed past action, contradicting 'Friday's meeting' which is still in the future

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you apply too much heat to glass too quickly, it ___ (crack) because the material expands unevenly.
distractor_rationale: will crack is incorrect because the clause 'because the material expands unevenly' explains a physical law that is always true, making this a zero conditional requiring present simple in both clauses
past simple cannot express a scientific principle that holds true universally and at all times
would crack belongs to the second conditional for hypothetical situations, but this is an established physical fact, not a hypothesis

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the technician does not fix the server issue by this evening, the entire department ___ (lose) access to its files overnight.
distractor_rationale: present simple in the result clause would make this a zero conditional universal truth, but 'by this evening' and 'overnight' mark a specific realistic future scenario requiring will
would lose belongs to the second conditional for unlikely or hypothetical situations, whereas this is presented as a genuine and realistic future risk
is going to lose, while sometimes acceptable for future plans, cannot function as the result clause of a first conditional — the standard structure requires will + base verb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you leave milk out of the fridge for too long, it ___ (go) bad.
distractor_rationale: will go is incorrect for zero conditional, which describes a universal scientific fact using present simple in both clauses
went is past simple and cannot express a timeless, always-true result
is going is present continuous and cannot describe a general, repeated outcome

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If Tom ___ (not study) for his exam tomorrow, he will almost certainly fail it.
distractor_rationale: will not study incorrectly places will inside the if-clause, which is not permitted in first conditional
did not study is past simple and cannot refer to a realistic future condition
is not studying is present continuous and cannot anchor a general first conditional condition about a future event

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the team wins their match on Saturday, the manager ___ (give) everyone the following Monday off.
distractor_rationale: gives uses present simple in the result clause, which only works for zero conditional universal truths, not a specific realistic future event on Saturday
gave is past simple and cannot express a future result that depends on a future condition
is giving is present continuous and cannot function as the result clause of a first conditional about a specific future outcome

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Every time you mix red and blue paint together, you ___ (get) purple — it never changes.
distractor_rationale: will get is incorrect because the phrase 'every time' combined with 'it never changes' signals a universal, scientific truth requiring zero conditional with present simple in both clauses
got is past simple and cannot express a timeless, repeatable outcome
are getting is present continuous and cannot describe a general, invariable result

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the landlord ___ (not repair) the heating by Friday, the tenants will contact the housing authority to make a formal complaint.
distractor_rationale: will not repair incorrectly inserts will into the if-clause, which is grammatically forbidden in first conditional regardless of the future time reference
has not repaired uses present perfect, which cannot set up a future condition with a specific deadline such as 'by Friday' in a first conditional if-clause
did not repair is past simple and cannot express a condition that has not yet occurred but is expected before a future deadline

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If you mix blue and yellow paint, you ___ green.
distractor_rationale: will get is incorrect in zero conditional result clauses describing universal scientific facts; present simple is required
got is past simple and cannot describe a timeless, repeatable result
would get belongs to the second conditional for hypothetical situations, not universal truths

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Sara finishes her homework early tonight, she ___ her favourite TV show.
distractor_rationale: watches is present simple, which describes habits or zero-conditional facts, not a specific realistic future outcome
watched is past simple and cannot refer to a future result
would watch belongs to the second conditional for unreal or imaginary situations, not realistic future possibilities

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Water ___ if you cool it to zero degrees Celsius.
distractor_rationale: will freeze introduces a future modal that is inappropriate for a universal scientific law described by zero conditional
froze is past simple and cannot state a timeless physical fact
would freeze signals a hypothetical second conditional, not a zero-conditional scientific truth

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If you ___ to the gym regularly, your fitness level improves over time.
distractor_rationale: will go is incorrect in the if-clause of any conditional; the if-clause always uses present simple
went is past simple and breaks the zero-conditional pattern of present + present for general truths
would go belongs in the if-clause of a second conditional for unreal situations, not a general health fact

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If the weather stays sunny this afternoon, we ___ a picnic in the park.
distractor_rationale: have is present simple, which implies a habitual or universal truth rather than a specific realistic future plan
had is past simple and cannot refer to a future event conditioned on this afternoon's weather
would have signals a second conditional for hypothetical or unreal situations, not a realistic future possibility

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If you ___ too much sugar in your coffee, it tastes very sweet.
distractor_rationale: will put is incorrect in the if-clause; the if-clause in both zero and first conditional requires present simple, never will
putting is a present participle and cannot function as the main verb in the if-clause without an auxiliary
would put belongs to the second conditional and cannot appear in the if-clause of a zero conditional describing a universal taste fact

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Mark doesn't leave the office by six o'clock this evening, he ___ the last train home.
distractor_rationale: misses is present simple, which implies a habitual or general truth rather than a specific realistic future consequence this evening
missed is past simple and cannot describe a future result that depends on tonight's actions
would miss belongs to the second conditional for imaginary scenarios, not a realistic and imminent future consequence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If you leave fresh milk outside the fridge on a warm day, it ___ within a few hours.
distractor_rationale: will go off introduces a future modal inappropriate for a zero conditional describing a universal biological fact about food spoilage
went off is past simple and cannot express a timeless, repeatable result that always happens under these conditions
would go off signals a second conditional for hypothetical situations, not a predictable scientific truth about milk

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If Anna submits her job application before the deadline on Friday, the manager ___ it by the end of next week.
distractor_rationale: reviews is present simple, which cannot express a specific future result that depends on Anna's action before Friday's deadline
reviewed is past simple and is incompatible with a future consequence occurring next week
is reviewing is present continuous, which describes an action already in progress now, not a future result contingent on Friday's submission

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If the team ___ to agree on a plan during tomorrow's meeting, the project deadline will almost certainly be delayed.
distractor_rationale: will fail is incorrect in the if-clause; the if-clause of a first conditional must use present simple, never will
failed is past simple and cannot appear in the if-clause of a first conditional referring to tomorrow's meeting
would fail belongs in the if-clause of a second conditional for unreal hypotheticals, not a realistic future risk about tomorrow

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If you heat ice, it melted.
content.error_sentence: If you heat ice, it melted.
content.corrected_sentence: If you heat ice, it melts.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If she will pass the exam next month, she gets a place at the college.
content.error_sentence: If she will pass the exam next month, she gets a place at the college.
content.corrected_sentence: If she passes the exam next month, she gets a place at the college.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If the bus is late this evening, I miss my connection at the central station.
content.error_sentence: If the bus is late this evening, I miss my connection at the central station.
content.corrected_sentence: If the bus is late this evening, I will miss my connection at the central station.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If you will leave food out overnight, bacteria grow on it very quickly.
content.error_sentence: If you will leave food out overnight, bacteria grow on it very quickly.
content.corrected_sentence: If you leave food out overnight, bacteria grow on it very quickly.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: When David gets a job offer from the company next week, he will need to decide quickly.
content.error_sentence: When David gets a job offer from the company next week, he will need to decide quickly.
content.corrected_sentence: If David gets a job offer from the company next week, he will need to decide quickly.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If the manager will approve the new schedule tomorrow, the whole team starts the project on Monday.
content.error_sentence: If the manager will approve the new schedule tomorrow, the whole team starts the project on Monday.
content.corrected_sentence: If the manager approves the new schedule tomorrow, the whole team starts the project on Monday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If you mix red and blue paint together, you will get purple.
content.error_sentence: If you mix red and blue paint together, you will get purple.
content.corrected_sentence: If you mix red and blue paint together, you get purple.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If Anna doesn't book the hotel room by Friday, she will found that all the good options are already taken.
content.error_sentence: If Anna doesn't book the hotel room by Friday, she will found that all the good options are already taken.
content.corrected_sentence: If Anna doesn't book the hotel room by Friday, she will find that all the good options are already taken.
